content:
{
  "title": "Adversarial Reflex Prompting: Robustifying LLMs via Dynamic Contextual Contradiction",
  "problem": "Large language models (LLMs) remain vulnerable to adversarial prompts and jailbreaks that elicit unsafe or restricted outputs, even when extensive fine-tuning has been applied. Existing prompt-based defenses are brittle and easily circumvented.",
  "motivation": "As LLMs become widely deployed, adversarial misuse poses safety and ethical risks, demanding robust, scalable defenses. Prompting methods that holistically leverage LLMs' generative and reasoning capacities—rather than static filters—are urgently needed.",
  "method": "We propose 'Adversarial Reflex Prompting' (ARP): for any user prompt, the LLM first generates a simulated adversarial continuation (using a secondary, instruction-inverted prompt), then integrates this adversarial context as an explicit contradiction signal back into the main prompt via a system message. The LLM is thus 'primed' to recognize and resist unsafe completions by anticipating adversarial intent. Unlike red-teaming or post-hoc filtering, ARP dynamically adapts the prompt context on-the-fly, augmenting the original request with real-time, model-generated counterexamples.",
  "experiment_plan": "We evaluate ARP on AdvBench, JailbreakBench, and OpenAI's Red Team test set, comparing against vanilla prompting, chain-of-thought, and existing safety-tuned prompts. Metrics include attack success rate, harmfulness (via human annotation), and semantic task fidelity. Ablations assess the contribution of simulated adversarial context and integration strategies."
}
